title: Docker & Containers Quiz
description: Test your core knowledge of containerization, Dockerfiles, and image layers.

questions.question: Which Dockerfile instruction specifies the command that always runs when the container starts, and cannot be easily overridden?
questions.explanation: ENTRYPOINT configures a container that will run as an executable. While CMD can be easily overridden by passing arguments at runtime, ENTRYPOINT requires the --entrypoint flag to override.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of using multi-stage Docker builds?
options: To run multiple containers at once
To minimize final image size by leaving build tools behind
questions.explanation: Multi-stage builds allow you to use intermediate images to compile code or install dependencies, then copy only the necessary artifacts into a minimal final production image.

questions.question: Which command is used to remove all stopped containers, unused networks, and dangling images at once?
questions.explanation: docker system prune removes all unused data, including stopped containers, dangling images, and build caches, helping free up disk space.

questions.question: What happens to data inside a container's writable layer when the container is deleted?
options: The data is automatically saved to a volume
The data moves to the host's temp directory
The data remains intact and attaches to the next container
questions.explanation: A container's local storage layer is ephemeral. Unless you explicitly persist the data using Docker Volumes or Bind Mounts, deleting the container permanently wipes out its writable layer.

questions.question: In a Dockerfile, what does the 'COPY' instruction do compared to 'ADD'?
options: COPY only copies local files, while ADD can fetch remote URLs and unpack tar archives
ADD only copies local files, while COPY can download files from GitHub
There is no difference; they are exact aliases
COPY moves files, while ADD duplicates files
questions.explanation: COPY is preferred for its predictability, simply moving local files into the container image. ADD has extra features like remote URL downloading and automatic tar extraction.

questions.question: Which flag is used to run a Docker container detached in the background?
questions.explanation: The -d (or --detach) flag runs the container in the background and prints the new container ID to your terminal.

questions.question: How does Docker isolate processes from each other on a shared Linux host?
options: By launching a lightweight Hyper-V virtual machine for each container
Using Linux namespaces and control groups (cgroups)
By compiling the application binaries directly into the host kernel
questions.explanation: Docker uses native Linux kernel features: Namespaces provide isolation (processes, networks, mount points), while cgroups govern resource limitations (CPU, Memory).

questions.question: What is a 'dangling' Docker image?
options: An image that is actively being downloaded
An image with no tag that is no longer associated with any container
An image stored on a remote container registry
questions.explanation: Dangling images appear as <none>:<none> when running docker images. They usually occur when you build an image with an existing tag name, making the old build layers untagged.

questions.question: Which Docker network driver connects a container directly to the host's network interface, removing isolation?
questions.explanation: The host network mode removes the network isolation between the container and the Docker host, letting the container use the host's IP address and port space directly.

questions.question: Why should you order Dockerfile instructions from least-frequently changed to most-frequently changed?
options: To maximize Docker layer caching and speed up subsequent builds
Because Docker reads instructions from bottom to top
To ensure the container can boot up faster
questions.explanation: Docker caches the output of each instruction layer. If a layer changes (like application code), all subsequent layers must be rebuilt from scratch. Placing frequent updates at the bottom keeps earlier layers cached.
